item.title: Glimpses of a Vim configuration
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
It's been a while since I startôÙed trackôÙing verôÙsions of my cusôÙtom Vim conôÙfigôÙuôÙraôÙtion, and makôÙing it availôÙable as an open source softôÙware in Github. The best of this project is, in my opinôÙion, to have it unôÙder verôÙsion conôÙtrol, so I can track changes and reôÙleasôÙes.

EvôÙery once in a while, when I find a new setôÙting, or a great new feaôÙture, I modôÙiôÙfy the conôÙfigôÙuôÙraôÙtion, so they will beôÙcome availôÙable on the next reôÙlease. BeôÙsides the feaôÙtures that are menôÙtioned in the proôÙjecôÙt, and the cusôÙtomizaôÙtions made, I feel very comôÙfortôÙable with the colour scheme I made.

Here are some glimpses of it:

First capôÙture of colours, and layôÙout

The colour scheme is genôÙerôÙal for the synôÙtax highôÙlightôÙing of all types recôÙogôÙnized by Vim. Please note this might alôÙso deôÙpend on the conôÙfigôÙuôÙraôÙtion of your terôÙmiôÙnal.

The tabs are alôÙso themed acôÙcordôÙing to the menus.

Any sugôÙgesôÙtions or imôÙproveôÙments to the code and conôÙfigôÙuôÙraôÙtion can be made on the Github projecôÙt.

item.title: Checking Python files with Vim
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
Vim is an exôÙcelôÙlent edôÙiôÙtor, and masôÙterôÙing it leads to more proôÙducôÙtivôÙiôÙty. Even though is very exôÙtenôÙsiôÙble and alôÙlows to be conôÙfigôÙured by many plug-ins I rather keep it as simôÙple as posôÙsiôÙble, tryôÙing not to use many plug-ins (neiôÙther packôÙagers like VunôÙdle, etc.).

HowôÙevôÙer, I do make use of an exôÙtenôÙsion that checks Python files for erôÙrors, PEP8, aôÙmong othôÙer things: flake8. BeôÙcause I do not use plug-in platôÙforms for Vim, I inôÙstall just this one manôÙuôÙalôÙly, by makôÙing the comôÙmand flake8 availôÙable sysôÙtem-wide [1].

Then the inôÙstalôÙlaôÙtion is as simôÙple as downôÙloadôÙing the project and coyôÙing the files inôÙto the ~/.vim/ftôÙôÙplugôÙin/python diôÙrecôÙtoôÙry. Make sure you have the folôÙlowôÙing line added on your ~/.vimôÙrc:

filetype plugin indent on

The feaôÙtures I use are mainôÙly the synôÙtax and PEP-8 comôÙpliôÙance checkôÙerôÙs. It can alôÙso warn you about unôÙused imôÙportôÙs, and cyôÙcloôÙmatôÙic comôÙplexôÙiôÙty.

It is useôÙful beôÙcause things like PEP-8 comôÙpliôÙance help to have a good code qualôÙiôÙty, and thereôÙfore a more readôÙable and mainôÙtainôÙable code base, speôÙcialôÙly on large projects with lots of files and modôÙules.

That's alôÙl. For more deôÙtails and othôÙer conôÙfigôÙuôÙraôÙtion tips checkôÙout my Vim setôÙup.

[1]Another option would be to install it on your virtual environment, but then you have to make sure to install it once per project. It is actually better, because you are not using the global system environment, but for packages like this, it should not be an issue, it's your choice.

item.title: Vim commands for improved productivity
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
I would like to deôÙscribe my favourite Vim comôÙmands that I use on a daiôÙly baôÙsis, in orôÙder to share some tips that could help you if you are new in this edôÙiôÙtor, or ôÙto imôÙprove your exôÙpeôÙriôÙence even if you use it.

  • J : UseôÙful when orôÙgaôÙnizôÙing code, this will join the line beôÙlow to the curôÙrent one.
  • ci) ("change inôÙside ')'): AcôÙtuôÙalôÙly, the closôÙing brackôÙet could be changed by any othôÙer thing (ôÙlike ']', '}', etc.). This will erase evôÙeryôÙthing withôÙin the brackôÙets and set you in inôÙsert modôÙe (the c could alôÙso be changed for d for exôÙamôÙple if you just want to delete). Again, this is very useôÙful when refacôÙtorôÙing code, if you want to change the paôÙramôÙeôÙters of a funcôÙtion defôÙiôÙniôÙtion, or whatôÙevôÙer is in a block, etc.
  • (sôÙeôÙlect some code with visôÙual mode and then) zf : will fold the seôÙlectôÙed code. zd for unôÙfoldôÙing.
  • % : alone or along with some othôÙer opôÙerôÙaôÙtor, is useôÙful for opôÙerôÙatôÙing with matchôÙing brackôÙets in the code. It will match the closôÙing brackôÙet of the one you have the curôÙsor in.
  • C or D : if you want to change or delete from the curôÙrent poôÙsiôÙtion up to the end of the line, reôÙspecôÙtiveôÙly.
  • t, (or any othôÙer charôÙacôÙter inôÙstead of comôÙma) will point you until that charôÙacôÙter. The good about this, is that is posôÙsiôÙble to chain it with othôÙer comôÙmandôÙs, for exôÙamôÙple: "ct," will change all the conôÙtent unôÙtil the next comôÙma.
  • < or > will inôÙdent the code folôÙlowôÙing the "arôÙrow" diôÙrecôÙtion (acôÙcordôÙing to what is set in shiftwidth).
  • = AuôÙtoôÙmatôÙiôÙcalôÙly inôÙdents code (useôÙful when highôÙlightôÙing code in visôÙual modôÙe).
  • w, e or b will point you to the next worôÙd, to the end of the word, or back to the preôÙviôÙous word, reôÙspecôÙtiveôÙly. The nice thing about these opôÙerôÙaôÙtors is when they work ôÙcomôÙbined with othôÙerôÙs, for exôÙamôÙple:
    • cw will change the next word.
    • db will delete the preôÙviôÙous word.
  • { or } for movôÙing up or down through paraôÙgraphôÙs, reôÙspecôÙtiveôÙly.

In adôÙdiôÙtion, note that you do not need to know all posôÙsiôÙble comôÙmandôÙs, just those that will help you with your norôÙmal acôÙtivôÙiôÙties. This means that is could be enough with a smalôÙlôÙ ôÙsubôÙset of all the feaôÙtures (the list I wrote is very short inôÙdeed). And this is preôÙciseôÙly the idea beôÙhind this post: to show how ôÙsome few comôÙmands apôÙplied in the right conôÙtexôÙt, might make you edôÙit faster.

All in alôÙl, Vim is a great edôÙiôÙtor, full of amazôÙing feaôÙtures. LearnôÙing it is acôÙtuôÙalôÙly worth it, in the sense that you will get an amazôÙing proôÙducôÙtivôÙiôÙty in reôÙturn (yôÙou'll type and edôÙit ôÙcode faster).
